This can also mean <vocabulary>northeast region</vocabulary> when talking about China or Japan, but for other countries (and for the compass direction), we'd use 北東.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
Remember how 始める means "to begin something"? 始め is its noun version and means "beginning," as in the start of an action or process. に marks a point in time. So 始めに means <vocabulary>in the beginning</vocabulary>. 

始めに can also mean <vocabulary>to begin with</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>to start with</vocabulary> when introducing an opening action, like 始めに、ウォーミングアップをしましょう (let's begin with some warm-up exercises).
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The reading is kun'yomi (look at all that hiragana!) but that's the reading you learned with the word 始める, so have no fear, you got it!

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
The <kanji>energy</kanji> you hold (持つ) within you is your <vocabulary>feeling</vocabulary>s. What kind of feeling is your energy giving you today?

気持ち can refer to your current mood, to a particular emotion inside you, or to physical feelings and sensations. You hear it in phrases like 気持ちいい (feels good), 気持ちわるい (feels gross), or 気持ちが分かる (I understand how you feel).
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: 気 is on'yomi and you learned that with the kanji. 持ち, though, is kun'yomi, which you learned with the word 持つ. One of those weird exception readings, but the ち on the end of 持ち is a clue that it uses the kun'yomi.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: A <kanji>language</kanji> about <kanji>thing</kanji>s. This is a confusing one. Just think of it more like someone talking about things, anything, really. Someone who just talks about things is someone who tells stories. So, this word means <vocabulary>tale</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>story</vocabulary> or <vocabulary>legend</vocabulary> (not the Tom Cruise one, though).
vocabulary.readingMnemonic:
Both kanji take the kun'yomi here. For 物, that's もの, which you learned with the vocabulary 物. You learned the kun'yomi for 語 with the verb 語る, but here it becomes がたり because of rendaku, and the り is tucked inside the kanji.

Maybe you've already heard the word ものがたり too. It's in some film and book names. If you haven't, and you find this reading hard to remember, think of a story about an infamous <reading>Gatling</reading> (がたり) gun that wandered the land, mowing everything down in its path. It's a terrible legend, but what if it were true???

The kanji itself means <kanji>transmit</kanji>, but 伝える is mostly used for "transmitting" information, feelings, or messages to someone else. So it means <vocabulary>to convey</vocabulary>, <vocabulary>to tell</vocabulary>, or <vocabulary>to pass down</vocabulary>.

In technical contexts, 伝える can also mean <vocabulary>to transmit</vocabulary>, in the sense that copper transmits heat or air transmits sound.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:

Other more technical meanings are <vocabulary>radio wave</vocabulary> and <vocabulary>electromagnetic waves</vocabulary>. If you already know of Shinto in English, though, this reading should be really easy. If you don't, perhaps you can use this reading to help you to remember Shinto the religion.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic:
You hopefully know the word 返す, which means "to return" (as in, you're doing the direct action of returning something). This word, on the other hand, is the version where something just returns (and you're not doing the action of returning it). Use the る to help you to remember this. る the Kangaroo is returned to you. You don't do any of the returning, it just happens or someone else does it. That's why this word means <vocabulary>to be returned</vocabulary>.

Note this word is often combined with くる (to come), which reinforces the aspect that something is coming back to you.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The reading is the same as 返す, so as long as you know that you'll be able to apply it here to learn the reading of this word as well.

vocabulary.meaningMnemonic: A "suffering hand" is a little misleading. Think more in terms of the words 苦い (bitter) and 手 (person... remember how that happens sometimes?). Now think of this word like you're bitter about having to work with someone. Why? Because they are extremely <vocabulary>hard to deal with</vocabulary>.
vocabulary.readingMnemonic: The reading is that of 苦い (にがい) plus regular old 手 (て). Remember, this is your bitter person, the person who's "hard to deal with." So, it uses the strange にがい reading.
